concerns.text: Section 5.2 (Analysis of Different Judges): The paper claims the Friedman test was used to compare judges (p-values 0.14-0.53). However, the Friedman test is for comparing >2 related samples. With only 3 judges (Gemini-3-Flash, Qwen3-VL-235B, Human), this is valid, but the text fails to report the test statistic (Chi-square or F) or the degrees of freedom, making the result irreproducible. Additionally, the table lists 'P-value' but the text says 'p-values... consistently exceeded 0.05'. The specific p-values for each metric (Rel, Ans) per model pair are missing, only a range is given. This obscures whether the non-significance holds for all comparisons or just the aggregate.

Section 5.2 & Table 2: The paper states '95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap resampling (N=1897 samples) for all Table 2 metrics'. However, Table 2 (main_results) and Table 3 (multi_scenario_results) do NOT display these confidence intervals. They only show point estimates. The text claims variance metrics are included to support superiority claims, but no error bars, CIs, or standard deviations are visible in the tables or figures. This renders the claim of 'statistical significance' for the 'best' and 'second-best' highlights unverifiable.

Section 5.2 & Table 2: The paper mentions 'pairwise bootstrap t-tests performed for top model comparisons'. It does not specify the null hypothesis, the number of permutations, or the correction method used for these specific pairwise tests (beyond the general Benjamini-Hochberg mention). Without the p-values for these specific pairwise comparisons (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro vs. Gemini-3-Flash), the claim that one is 'best' over the other is not statistically substantiated in the text.

Section 3.3 (Quality Control): The 'Crucial Evidence' identification uses an ablation procedure with a single model (Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B). The paper does not report the stability or variance of this identification process. If the 'Crucial Evidence' set is noisy or unstable, the Recall metric (which depends on it) becomes unreliable. A statistical measure of inter-annotator agreement (if multiple models were used) or a sensitivity analysis of the ablation threshold is missing.

Section 5.1 (Main Results): The paper highlights 'best' and 'second-best' results in Table 2 using color. It states 'Multiple-comparisons correction (Benjamini-Hochberg FDR, q<0.05) applied'. However, the table does not indicate which differences are statistically significant after correction. A model might be 'best' in point estimate but not significantly different from the 'second-best' after FDR correction. The visual highlighting implies a definitive ranking that the statistical test might not support.
